tweets.full_text:
【衝撃】
Claude Codeが僕の「考える仕事」を変えた瞬間

コードを書かない日が増えた。
でも頭の中は以前よりずっと忙しい。

AIが実装の細かい部分を握ると、
僕は「何を作るか」にだけ集中できる。

不思議なことに、
手を動かすよりも思考が深くなっている気がする。

これまでの僕：手を動かしてコードを書く。
今の僕：頭を動かして設計しAIに任せる。

でもそれは、
創造性がAIに奪われるのではなく、
むしろ広がっている瞬間だった。

AIにコードを「書かせる」のではなく、
僕の思考が「コードになる」感覚。

誰かに伝わってほしい、
この曖昧だけど確かな変化。

Claude Codeと一緒に働くことは、
プログラマーの新しい存在証明だと思う。

tweets.full_text:
🔄 AIコーディングの使い方を「コード生成」から「エンジニアリングプロセス全体の最適化」へ引き上げるプラグインが登場しました。37スキル・51エージェントでワークフロー全体をカバーしています。

タイトル: everyinc/compound-engineering-plugin
URL: https://t.co/kfFAYpQfIn

💡 概要
Claude Code、Cursor、Codex、Copilot CLI等の主要AI開発ツールに対応した統合ワークフロープラグインです。「各エンジニアリング作業の単位が、次の作業をより簡単にすべきであり、難しくしてはならない」という複利的思考のもと、計画（80%）と実行（20%）の比率を重視した開発プロセスを実現します。

❓ 解決する課題
AIコーディングツールは「速くコードを書く」には優れていますが、戦略策定、要件整理、実装計画、コードレビュー、学習の蓄積といったエンジニアリングプロセス全体をカバーするワークフローが不足していました。また各ツールのプラグイン形式が異なるため、横断的に同じワークフローを使うことが困難でした。

🛠 方法論と提案手法
・コアワークフローループとして、/ce-strategy（製品方針策定）→ /ce-ideate（アイデア生成・評価）→ /ce-brainstorm（対話的要件整理）→ /ce-plan（詳細実装計画）→ /ce-work（タスク実行・進捗追跡）→ /ce-debug（体系的バグ調査）→ /ce-code-review（マルチエージェントレビュー）→ /ce-compound（学習の文書化）を提供しています
・/ce-product-pulseでタイムウィンドウ付きの使用状況・パフォーマンスレポートを生成し、データに基づく意思決定を支援します
・STRATEGY.md、要件ドキュメント、パルスレポートを組織の記憶として自動生成・蓄積します
・TypeScript/Bunベースのコンバータアーキテクチャにより、Claude Code、Cursor、Codex、Copilot CLI、OpenCode、Pi、Gemini、Kiroなど多様なターゲットへの形式変換をサポートしています

🎯 ユースケース
・AIコーディングツールを使った開発プロジェクトの体系的な計画・実行・レビュー・知識蓄積サイクルの構築に活用できます
・複数の専門エージェント（レビュアー、リサーチャー等）への並列委任によるコードレビューの自動化に有用です
・複数のAI開発ツール間で統一的なワークフローを維持したいチームに適しています
・プロジェクトの学習を文書化して将来の作業に活かす継続的改善プロセスの構築に使えます

📊 実績
・GitHub上で20kスターを獲得し、843コミットの活発な開発が行われています
・37スキル、51エージェントを搭載し、エンジニアリングプロセスのあらゆる段階をカバーしています
・npmパッケージ（@every-env/compound-plugin）として公開されており、MITライセンスで利用可能です

tweets.full_text:
Some of Mo's claims here are right-ish, but I think it takes him to the wrong conclusion. 

Yes, AI is super expensive to serve. And there will be a reckoning when everyone is forced to pay full price.

And there will also likely be a major correction around insane OpenAI-style financing. With trillions of dollars flying around being shell-game-passed between companies. Seems like there's some sort of bubble there for sure.

But what he's done here is gloss over the fact that he's claiming certainty about outcomes in a world where it's hard to predict anything.

Just because the AI is expensive doesn't mean it didn't move developers from "I'd never use it for code" to "I don't code myself anymore" in less than a calendar year.

And we've not even seen companies start to adopt AI. Companies suck at change, so it'll take a long time. You can't just demand people use it and suddenly get it integrated into processes and workflows. Most companies aren't anywhere near ready for AI.

And then there's the fact that any moment AI could hit another jump, or discover something, that changes the game again. GPT-4 style. Or Claude Code style.

But I think the biggest flaw in this narrative is that the world is now addicted to AI, so they won't want to give it up. 

I think there is about to be extreme downward pressure on inference costs due to innovations in chips, algorithms, and a million other small innovations (custom chips being a big one). 

There is zero reason to believe that our current level of efficiency at doing inference is anywhere near a stable plateau given the fact that this entire science is basically being invented in realtime. Researchers have been telling us for years how kludgy and inefficient the research process is, and how much slack is left in the rope.

I am an optimist, so I have some bias towards this, but I think reality also shares this bias towards slack in the rope when a discipline is so incredibly young.

In short, if/when inference cost drops significantly, or some major traction is made inside companies through like a digital employee product that actually works, or AI discovers some major human-enhancing drug, or a dozen other potential developments...the train not only continues but accelerates.

I think an AI investment crash is definitely possible, if the timing hits a certain way. And the clock is ticking on that.

But there are many scenarios that would either extend the time on that clock, or stop it altogether.

Once the bubble pops, Anthropic and OpenAI will become the Coinbase and Block of the AI world. Mundane companies that ship narrative wrappers on mundane bytes.

That the bubble will pop isn’t some apocalyptic doomsday prophecy. It’s not that complicated: AI is freakishly expensive to serve. If the returns on the other end are not justified, the bubble pops. And thus begins the decades long buildout to actually economically justifiable AI.

It’s amusing how resistant reality is to our fictions and fantasies. In the peak of the crypto bubble we thought reality was going to be transformed into financial liberty and democratization for all, and network states and decentralized reserve currencies. Coinbase stood to be a multi-trillion dollar company and is now just a mundane tech startup.

Today we spin similar narratives about the intellectual upheaval of AI, about the new democratization of intelligence and how everything will soon begin to orbit this new technology.

At the end, Anthropic and OpenAI will be mundane IT providers with an insanely grim research outlook to make AI economically sensible and useful, no different from Google’s position in trying to make quantum commercially viable.

Reality is, fortunately, pretty hardened against our delusions.

tweets.full_text: Get ahead of the game by trading faster than your competitors with Hyperliquid markets accessible from your terminal or Claude Code without the hassle of private keys or complicated code

I locked Claude in a box with some detailed specifications and docs, and then went for a weekend with the family. 

Set up one continuous orchestrator that watches new commits and provides review, which are dropped in the dev orchestrators context after it finishes a task. They work in a continuous loop until the dev orchestrator provided no new commits for 15 minutes.

Checking in periodically on the progress and results; it's still running and the progress is surprisingly good so far. The token spend is not catastrophic despite multiple workflows and high thinking budget (still <50% weekly limit on 20x plan).

I've been doing tests like this about once a month where I just provide some obscene task and see where the limits are; what can be built in a finite amount of time. Then I informally evaluate the functionality, adherence to the design intent, and quality. The results of these are never published; sometimes they are related to my hobbies but sometimes just throwaway "what if" experiments/ideas that we discuss internally at Tabular Editor - since about February they are long-running or autonomous tasks.

Up until about November the results of these tests have always been sloppy on all fronts, and tbh I also hadnt been very serious or disciplined about my workflow or approach. Then (after Opus 4.5) suddenly the functionality was there, but the quality not at all. Slowly over months it's been able to do more and more and more... and go further, but not do better.

Something seems to have changed in the last 1-2 months though. I'm noticing in smaller experiments significantly better outputs, around since I've spent a bunch of effort trying to really lock things down and clean up my workflow... Trying to ensure consistency, security, and scalability. I'm not sure if that's why, but it probably helps. I just don't know how much.

But this test already seems to be showing a huge leap in progress and even possibly quality compared to the last time I tried a big experiment like this. I'm still skeptical of the clanker though and had to intervene once already to steer away from a pileup... but so far quite surprised. It's making me re-think a lot of things, again.

I'm not sure, but the new feature in Claude code of dynamic workflows seem to be a huge change in the right context, especially with skills that you tune the right way, and custom tools that you've tailored made to your scenario. I don't yet know though if it's just the change to my setup that's making the difference.

Some things I'm settling on as I try to find sleep

- lock down your memory and maintain it well. Don't be afraid to fill it temporarily with relevant info. Never let ai curated it, give the ai an obsidian vault or agent docs repo.

- You should use skills.
- Don't just make technical skills for technical processes and formats /frameworks. Skills work best when you compliment them with conceptual and nontechnical process context. Routing skills seem great too but I'm not sure yet.
- Don't just use skills ootb from a vendor. Take a skill or template that you like, fork it, and own it.
- Don't use skills to replace tools, that's stupid.
- You should test skills but this is really fucking hard and tbh I don't think I've come up with evals yet for this that doesn't feel like hand wavy bs

- don't use ai to make plans or create context. The human should own it, the ai can iterate and clean it or refine it.

- /goal is incredible if you know you have a complete feedback loop that you verified. /loop and /schedule feels icky.

tweets.full_text:
🛠️🧭How to Use Claude Code to Build Production AI Agents → Even If you're Starting from Zero.
𝗧𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗶𝘀 𝗺𝘆 𝟵-𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗽 𝗿𝗼𝗮𝗱𝗺𝗮𝗽 𝗳𝗿𝗼𝗺 𝗕𝗠𝗔𝗗 𝗽𝗹𝗮𝗻𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗼 /𝗲𝘃𝗮𝗹𝘀-𝗰𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘁𝗲-𝘀𝘂𝗶𝘁𝗲.

Most people open Claude Code and ask it to build.
I don’t.
I make it plan, structure, isolate, test, remember, and evaluate first.

Here is my workflow:

》𝗦𝘁𝗲𝗽 𝟭: 𝗕𝗠𝗔𝗗 𝗣𝗹𝗮𝗻𝗻𝗶𝗻𝗴
 Start with the agent’s work, not the code.
✸ Use Skill /pm + /analyst from BMAD
✸ Define user, task, constraints, success
✸ Turn ideas into a buildable spec
→ Example: You will never forget edge cases, gaurrails, success metrics.

》𝗦𝘁𝗲𝗽 𝟮: 𝗖𝗟𝗔𝗨𝗗𝗘.𝗺𝗱
 Give Claude rules before files.
✸ Critical rules first
✸ What must never break
✸ Blunt, specific, concrete
→ Example: One rule can avoid mistakes looping for hours.

》𝗦𝘁𝗲𝗽 𝟯: /𝗖𝗟𝗘𝗔𝗥 + 𝗣𝗹𝗮𝗻 𝗠𝗼𝗱𝗲
 Stale context breaks agents.
✸ Clear old assumptions
✸ Plan before coding
✸ Review flow, tools, schemas
→ Example: Wrong framework? /clear. Fresh plan. Clean start.

》𝗦𝘁𝗲𝗽 𝟰: 𝗦𝘁𝗿𝘂𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲𝗱 𝗜𝗻𝗽𝘂𝘁𝘀 & 𝗢𝘂𝘁𝗽𝘂𝘁𝘀
 Messy output means fragile agents.
✸ Use Pydantic or JSON schema
✸ Make tools act like APIs
✸ Avoid free-form outputs
→ Example: Return answer, source, confidence, next action.

》𝗦𝘁𝗲𝗽 𝟱: 𝗦𝗸𝗶𝗹𝗹𝘀, 𝗧𝗼𝗼𝗹𝘀 & 𝗠𝗖𝗣
 Do not rebuild what exists.
✸ Use Skills for repeated work
✸ Add tools and MCP servers
✸ Integrate before custom code
→ Example: Do not paste docs. Connect context once.

》𝗦𝘁𝗲𝗽 𝟲: 𝗦𝘂𝗯-𝗔𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘀 & 𝗪𝗼𝗿𝗸𝘁𝗿𝗲𝗲𝘀
 One session should not do everything.
✸ Split research, build, test
✸ One git worktree per task
✸ Run in parallel safely
→ Example: One Sub-agent researches. One builds. One tests.

》𝗦𝘁𝗲𝗽 𝟳: 𝗛𝗼𝗼𝗸𝘀
 Hooks make Claude safer.
✸ Block risky actions
✸ Run tests automatically
✸ Check schemas, lint, permissions
→ Example: Tests fail. Hook runs. Claude fixes.

》𝗦𝘁𝗲𝗽 𝟴: 𝗠𝗲𝗺𝗼𝗿𝘆 & 𝗥𝗔𝗚 𝗦𝗸𝗶𝗹𝗹𝘀
 Use trusted context, not random memory.
✸ Use Skill /memory-reviewer from Memo0
✸ Use Skill /rag-engineer from antigravity-awesome-skills
✸ Store decisions and trusted context
→ Example: Claude remembers the decision.

》𝗦𝘁𝗲𝗽 𝟵: /𝗲𝘃𝗮𝗹𝘀-𝗰𝗿𝗲𝗮𝘁𝗲-𝘀𝘂𝗶𝘁𝗲
 If you cannot measure it, it is not production.
✸ Use Skill /evals-create-suite from elastic/kibana
✸ Test happy paths and failures
✸ Measure before shipping
→ Example: No “looks good.” Pass or fail.

𝗣𝗹𝗲𝗮𝘀𝗲 𝗥𝗲𝗺𝗲𝗺𝗯𝗲𝗿:
Yes. Claude Code speeds up coding.
Yes. Skills reduce repetition.

But production AI agent building is still the same discipline:
Plan → Structure → Build → Isolate → Guardrail → Ground → Evaluate.

Without good architecture, Claude or any AI Coding build fragile agents, just faster.

I FOUND OUT HOW PEOPLE BUILD WEBSITES THAT LOOK LIKE THEY COST THOUSANDS, USING CLAUDE DESIGN. FULL GUIDE

A studio rebuilt its entire website in four hours. Not a template, a site that looks like a $10k agency made it

The trick isn't one tool, it's the chain: design in one, build in another, animate, ship. Here's the exact workflow

The exact process:

1. Build the design system first. Take your brand from Figma and drop it into Claude Design. It generates a full design system, spacing, shadows, components, all of it. This is the foundation everything else sits on

2. Hand it to Claude Code. Export the design system and bring it into Claude Code. This is where the actual site gets built, with real code, not a locked editor

3. Plan the architecture before building. Brainstorm the site structure with Claude first, built around conversions, your offers and your values. Don't let it build blind

4. Install the frontend-design skill. This is the step that stops your site looking like every other AI-generated page. It forces a real design direction instead of generic output. Drop in your custom assets too, then let Claude build with all that context. Refine until it's perfect

5. Add animation, then launch. Install the Remotion skill to add custom animations that make the site feel premium. When it's ready, push to Vercel and the site is live in about a minute

The reason it looks expensive isn't the AI. It's the design system going in first and the skill keeping it off the generic path
